eliminated.name: Hassan Nasrallah
eliminated.title: Secretary-General of Hezbollah
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force bunker-buster strike
eliminated.significance: Led Hezbollah for 32 years since 1992. Transformed Hezbollah into the most powerful non-state military force in the Middle East. Under his leadership, Hezbollah amassed 150,000+ rockets and missiles. His elimination marked the most significant blow to Iran's proxy network in decades.

eliminated.name: Ismail Haniyeh
eliminated.title: Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau
eliminated.method: Targeted assassination — explosive device in Tehran guesthouse
eliminated.significance: Head of Hamas' political bureau since 2017. Former Prime Minister of the Palestinian Authority (2006-2007). Served as Hamas' chief diplomat and fundraiser, managing relationships with Iran, Qatar, and Turkey. Killed while attending the inauguration of Iran's new president.

eliminated.name: Fuad Shukr
eliminated.title: Senior Military Commander, Hezbollah
eliminated.significance: Hezbollah's most senior military commander after Nasrallah. Responsible for Hezbollah's strategic weapons program. US had a $5 million Rewards for Justice bounty on him. Linked to the 1983 Beirut barracks bombing that killed 241 US Marines.

eliminated.name: Saleh al-Arouri
eliminated.title: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau; Founder of West Bank military infrastructure
eliminated.method: Airstrike — drone strike attributed to Israel
eliminated.significance: Founded Hamas' military operations in the West Bank. Key liaison between Hamas and Hezbollah in Lebanon. Believed to be involved in planning the October 7 attack. His killing in Beirut demonstrated Israel's reach into Hezbollah's stronghold.

eliminated.name: Ayman al-Zawahiri
eliminated.title: Emir (Leader) of Al-Qaeda
eliminated.method: CIA drone strike — R9X Hellfire missile (kinetic, no warhead)
eliminated.operationName: CIA operation — authorized by President Biden
eliminated.significance: Succeeded Osama bin Laden as Al-Qaeda leader in 2011. Egyptian surgeon who co-founded Egyptian Islamic Jihad. Key planner of 9/11 attacks. His presence in Taliban-controlled Kabul proved the Taliban had not severed ties with Al-Qaeda as promised in the 2020 Doha Agreement.

eliminated.name: Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi
eliminated.title: Caliph / Leader of the Islamic State
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — Delta Force (detonated suicide vest during raid)
eliminated.significance: Declared the ISIS 'caliphate' from the Great Mosque of al-Nuri in Mosul, June 2014. Under his leadership, ISIS controlled territory the size of Britain, generated $1B+ annually, and inspired terrorist attacks worldwide. The most wanted terrorist in the world at the time of his death.

eliminated.name: Qasem Soleimani
eliminated.title: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force
eliminated.method: US drone strike — MQ-9 Reaper firing Hellfire missiles
eliminated.significance: Iran's most powerful military figure. Architect of Iran's proxy network across the Middle East. Directed operations by Hezbollah, Shia militias in Iraq, Houthis, and Assad regime support. His assassination by the US was the most significant targeted killing since Osama bin Laden. Nearly triggered direct US-Iran war.

eliminated.name: Osama bin Laden
eliminated.title: Founder and Emir of Al-Qaeda
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — SEAL Team Six (DEVGRU)
eliminated.significance: Mastermind of the September 11, 2001 attacks that killed 2,977 people. Founded Al-Qaeda in 1988. The most wanted person in the world for nearly a decade. His killing by Navy SEALs in a compound near Pakistan's military academy was one of the most significant special operations in history.

eliminated.name: Abu Ibrahim al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi
eliminated.title: Leader of the Islamic State (2nd Caliph)
eliminated.method: US Special Operations raid — detonated suicide vest
eliminated.operationName: US counterterrorism operation — authorized by President Biden
eliminated.significance: Succeeded al-Baghdadi as ISIS leader. Maintained the organization's global insurgency network despite territorial losses. Killed in a US raid similar to the al-Baghdadi operation.

eliminated.name: Ibrahim Aqil
eliminated.title: Commander of Hezbollah's Radwan Force (elite special operations unit)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force precision strike on Beirut
eliminated.operationName: Israeli targeted strike during Hezbollah military meeting
eliminated.significance: Commander of Hezbollah's elite Radwan Force. US had a $7 million bounty for his role in the 1983 US Embassy bombing and Marine barracks bombing in Beirut. Killed alongside multiple other Radwan Force commanders in what Israel described as a strike on a meeting of senior military leadership.

eliminated.name: Yevgeny Prigozhin
eliminated.title: Founder and Leader of Wagner Group
eliminated.method: Aircraft crash — Embraer Legacy business jet shot down (alleged assassination)
eliminated.location: Tver Oblast, Russia (in-flight between Moscow and St. Petersburg)
eliminated.operationName: Widely attributed to Russian state / GRU
eliminated.significance: Founded the Wagner Group private military company. Led the failed June 2023 mutiny against Russian military leadership. Exactly two months after the mutiny, his plane crashed killing all aboard. His death consolidated Russian state control over Wagner operations, which were rebranded as 'Africa Corps' under GRU command.

eliminated.name: Mohammed Deif
eliminated.title: Commander of the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades (Hamas military wing)
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike using multiple munitions
eliminated.significance: Supreme commander of Hamas' military wing for over 20 years. Mastermind of the October 7, 2023 attack. Survived at least 7 previous Israeli assassination attempts, losing an eye, an arm, and the ability to walk. The most wanted person in Israel for decades.

eliminated.name: Marwan Issa
eliminated.title: Deputy Commander of Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground compound
eliminated.significance: Known as the 'Shadow Man' — Hamas' third most senior military leader. Deputy to Mohammed Deif. Key planner of the October 7 attack. Oversaw Hamas' tunnel network and weapons development programs.

eliminated.name: Abu Hussein al-Hamidawi
eliminated.title: Leader of the Islamic State (4th)
eliminated.significance: Succeeded Abu al-Hassan al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi as ISIS leader. Led ISIS for approximately 7 months before being killed, demonstrating the group's difficulty in maintaining stable leadership under constant pressure from US and allied forces.

eliminated.name: Hashem Safieddine
eliminated.title: Head of Hezbollah's Executive Council; Expected successor to Nasrallah
eliminated.method: Airstrike — Israeli Air Force strike on underground bunker in Beirut
eliminated.significance: Nasrallah's cousin and the most likely successor to lead Hezbollah. Headed Hezbollah's Executive Council, managing the organization's financial and administrative operations. His killing days after Nasrallah's assassination further decapitated Hezbollah's leadership structure.

eliminated.name: Abubakar Shekau
eliminated.title: Leader of Boko Haram
eliminated.method: Suicide — detonated explosive vest during ISWAP assault on his position
eliminated.operationName: ISWAP assault on Boko Haram stronghold
eliminated.significance: Notorious leader of Boko Haram since 2009. Orchestrated the 2014 kidnapping of 276 Chibok schoolgirls. Under his leadership, Boko Haram killed over 20,000 people. Killed himself with a suicide vest rather than surrender to rival ISWAP fighters, leading to Boko Haram's fragmentation.

active.name: Yahya Sinwar
active.title: Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau (succeeded Haniyeh); Leader of Hamas in Gaza
active.description: Mastermind and operational commander of the October 7, 2023 attack. Rose through Hamas' ranks in Israeli prison where he served 22 years. Known as 'The Butcher of Khan Younis.' Israel's #1 most wanted individual. Believed to be alive in Gaza's tunnel network, possibly using hostages as human shields. Appointed head of Hamas political bureau after Haniyeh's assassination.

active.name: Ali Khamenei
active.title: Supreme Leader of Iran; Commander-in-Chief of all Iranian armed forces including IRGC
active.description: Supreme Leader of Iran since 1989. Ultimate authority over the IRGC and Iran's proxy network. Authorized the April 2024 direct missile and drone attack on Israel — the first direct Iranian strike on Israeli territory. Controls Iran's nuclear program and 'Axis of Resistance' strategy.

active.name: Esmail Qaani
active.title: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (succeeded Soleimani)
active.description: Succeeded Qasem Soleimani as commander of the IRGC Quds Force. Manages Iran's proxy relationships across the Middle East. Less charismatic and operationally aggressive than Soleimani but maintains the proxy network. Reportedly suffered a cardiac event in late 2024 and his current operational status is unclear.
knownAssociates: Iranian proxy militia commanders across Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Yemen

active.name: Abdul Malik al-Houthi
active.title: Leader of the Houthi movement / Ansar Allah
active.lastKnownLocation: Undisclosed location in northern Yemen (Saada region)
active.description: Political and religious leader of the Houthi movement since 2004 (following his brother Hussein's death). Commands the Houthi forces conducting the Red Sea anti-shipping campaign. Portrays himself as defender of Palestinian cause. Has escalated attacks despite US/UK military strikes on Houthi positions.

active.name: Naim Qassem
active.title: Secretary-General of Hezbollah (succeeded Nasrallah, elected October 2024)
active.description: Long-time deputy to Hassan Nasrallah. Elected as Hezbollah's new Secretary-General following the assassinations of Nasrallah and Safieddine. Theologian and political figure rather than military commander. Faces the challenge of rebuilding Hezbollah's command structure after Israel's decapitation campaign.

active.name: Abu Hafs al-Hashimi al-Qurayshi
active.title: Leader of the Islamic State (current)
active.description: Current leader of ISIS, announced in August 2023. Little is publicly known about his identity or background. Leads an organization that continues to conduct insurgent attacks in Syria and Iraq while maintaining global affiliate networks across Africa and Central Asia.

active.name: Ahmed Diriye
active.title: Emir (Leader) of Al-Shabaab
active.description: Leader of Al-Shabaab since 2014, formally pledged allegiance to Al-Qaeda. Has expanded Al-Shabaab's revenue base and territorial control despite sustained US and AMISOM military pressure. Believed to be in hiding in southern Somalia with limited public appearances.

active.name: Iyad ag Ghaly
active.title: Leader of JNIM (Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin)
active.description: Tuareg leader who founded Ansar Dine and now leads JNIM, Al-Qaeda's Sahel affiliate. Former Malian diplomat turned jihadist. Has unified multiple jihadist factions across the Sahel into the most potent insurgent force in the region. The most wanted person in West Africa.

active.name: Saif al-Adel
active.title: De facto leader of Al-Qaeda (unconfirmed formal appointment)
active.lastKnownLocation: Iran (under IRGC monitoring / loose house arrest since 2003)
active.description: Egyptian former special forces officer. Al-Qaeda's military chief and likely successor to al-Zawahiri. Has been in Iran since 2003 under a complex arrangement with the IRGC. Wanted for his role in the 1998 US Embassy bombings in Kenya and Tanzania. If confirmed as Al-Qaeda's leader, he represents a unique case of a terrorist leader operating from rival Iran's territory.

active.name: Hibatullah Akhundzada
active.title: Supreme Leader (Amir al-Mu'minin) of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan
active.description: Supreme leader of the Taliban since 2016 and de facto ruler of Afghanistan since the August 2021 takeover. Reclusive Islamic scholar who has made very few public appearances. Has imposed the world's most restrictive policies on women and girls, banning secondary education and most employment. Rules through a small circle of Kandahari clerics.

active.name: Sirajuddin Haqqani
active.title: Minister of Interior, Taliban government; Leader of the Haqqani Network
active.description: Leader of the Haqqani Network, designated by the US as a Foreign Terrorist Organization. Now serves as Afghanistan's Interior Minister. The Haqqani Network has been responsible for some of the deadliest attacks in Afghanistan, including the 2008 Indian embassy bombing in Kabul and multiple attacks on US forces. He is a US-designated terrorist who now runs a government ministry.

active.name: Noor Wali Mehsud
active.title: Emir (Leader) of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP)
active.description: Leader of the TTP since June 2018. Has reunified TTP splinter factions and intensified attacks against Pakistan, especially since the Taliban takeover of Afghanistan which provided TTP with sanctuary. Pakistan accuses the Afghan Taliban of harboring TTP fighters despite Taliban denials.
